table_system.purpose: Unified rules for data tables across public comparison pages and admin dashboard interfaces.
scope: blog/admin data lists rendered as tables
core_principles: Tables are for high-density scanning, comparison, and action-taking.
The user must always understand column meaning, row grouping, and action location.
Tables must remain readable under real data, not just ideal demo content.
Table behavior must be predictable across pages.

comparison_table.purpose: Side-by-side or row-based evaluation of products/specs.

admin_data_table.purpose: Operational editing, reviewing, filtering, and managing data.

read_only_reference_table.purpose: Display static or informational tabular content.

rules: All wide tables must live inside a dedicated overflow wrapper.
Use overflow-x: auto for horizontally dense content.
Avoid allowing the full page layout to break because of table width.

width_behavior: Tables should use the available width of their container.
Do not force awkward compressed columns if scrolling is a better usability tradeoff.

rules: Use semantic table markup when the content is truly tabular.
Use cards or lists instead of tables if the content is highly unstructured on mobile-only contexts.
Column intent must remain clear from the header row.
Headers must be visually distinct from row data.
Use stronger contrast or weight than body cells.
Header labels must be short, literal, and unambiguous.
Sticky headers are recommended for tall scrollable tables.
Sticky headers must preserve readable background and stacking context.
Use sticky headers when vertical scanning spans many rows.
Sticky headers must not obscure content.
Use stable background color on sticky headers.
Rows must remain visually separable.
Use subtle borders, striping, or spacing rhythm to support scanning.
Hover state should aid tracking but remain subtle.
Selected row state must be clearly visible.
Do not make rows so tall that density suffers unnecessarily.

alignment_rules: Text content aligns left by default.
Short status indicators may center if consistent.
overflow_rules: Long text should use a defined strategy: truncate, wrap, or expand.
Do not let uncontrolled long strings break layout.
If truncating, preserve access to the full value via title, tooltip, or detail view when appropriate.
spacing_rules: Dense admin tables may use tighter padding than public comparison tables.

rules: Action columns should be placed predictably, usually last.
Identifier columns should remain easy to scan.
Columns with similar data types should align consistently across tables.
Avoid too many narrow columns that create unreadable clutter.
Action controls should not wrap unpredictably.
Use compact controls when space is constrained.
Keep row-level actions visually subordinate to table content unless action is the row's primary purpose.
Sorting and filtering controls belong in a table toolbar or nearby control zone, not inside random cells.
Sort state must be visible.
Filter state must be understandable and reversible.
Do not style sort or view toggles as primary CTAs.
Bulk selection controls must be obvious and consistent.
Bulk action bars should appear only when relevant.
Destructive bulk actions must require clear confirmation.
Selection state must remain visible during scrolling.

principles: Preserve table usefulness on small screens without destroying structure.
Horizontal scrolling is preferable to unreadable wrapping for dense datasets.
rules: Use overflow wrappers on smaller screens.
Keep important columns visible when possible.
Do not force multi-line action buttons inside narrow cells if it harms readability.
For extremely dense admin tables, retain table structure with scroll rather than transforming everything into cards.

comparison_table_rules.purpose: Rules specific to public-facing comparison tables.
rules: Key attributes should be easy to compare row-to-row or column-to-column.
Internal research actions such as View Details must be visually distinct from affiliate actions such as Check Price.
Comparison controls must not visually compete with page-level CTAs.
Spec labels must be muted relative to values while remaining legible.

admin_table_rules.purpose: Rules specific to admin dashboard tables.
rules: Favor compact density with readable headers and predictable action placement.
Admin tables may use sticky headers more aggressively.
Use stronger row-state affordances for hover and selection than public tables.
Preserve editing efficiency over decorative styling.
Status chips or labels inside tables must use a consistent semantic system.
Do not rely on color alone to communicate status.
Statuses should be short and scannable.
Use skeleton rows or loading placeholders that approximate final structure.
Avoid large layout shifts between loading and loaded state.
Do not show a blank area with only 'Loading...' if a structured skeleton is feasible.
If a table has no rows, show a meaningful empty state instead of an empty shell.
Empty states must explain whether there is no data, no search result, or no content yet.
Provide a recovery action when possible.
Table load failures must communicate the problem clearly.
Show retry or recovery actions when appropriate.
Do not leave broken headers or half-rendered rows without explanation.
Header text must be slightly stronger than body text.
Dense tables may use smaller type, but not below comfortable readability.
Line height should support scanning, not paragraph reading.
Avoid mixing too many weights inside a single table.
Use borders, background contrast, and spacing to create structure.
Do not overuse heavy shadows inside tables.
Visual cues should aid scanning without making the table noisy.
Use semantic table markup for true tables.
Header-to-cell relationships must remain understandable.
Focus-visible states must be visible on interactive cells or controls.
Do not rely only on hover to reveal critical actions.

anti_patterns: Action buttons scattered across random columns
Headers that look like body text
Blank tables with no empty state
Sorting controls styled like primary CTAs
implementation_notes_for_ai: Always define table wrapper behavior first.
Use the same action placement pattern across similar tables.
Keep internal detail actions and outbound affiliate actions visually distinct.
When in doubt, favor predictable density and scanning over decorative layout.
